title: Hi, I’m Adriana!
text:
Github
  
  
    
     LinkedIn
  
  
    
     Email
  




Hi, I’m Adriana!
I am an interdisciplinary psychology and language science graduate researcher in the Bilingualism and Linguistic Diversity Lab at The Pennsylvania State University (PI Janet van Hell). I use a combination of behavioral and neurocognitive methods (mainly EEG) to study how we learn, understand, and communicate novel meanings using language. Specifically, I am interested in topics such as novel word learning, creative language production, and novel metaphor comprehension. I focus on monolingual as well bilingual populations, and have a particular interest for second language learning and comprehension.
I am currently in my 6th year as a graduate student in Psychology and Language Science with a Specialization in Cognitive and Affective Neuroscience at Penn State. I earned my M.S. in Psychology from Penn State in 2023. I completed my B.A. in Psychology and Sociology, with a minor in Global Studies from the University of Nebraska-Lincoln (summa cum laude) in 2021.
In addition to my research, I also enjoy teaching and mentoring. I taught an upper-level psychology class about my favorite subject—Language and Thought—in Fall 2025, and am currently preparing to teach PSYCH 100 this fall! I am also lucky to get to work with many amazing undergraduate research assistants in the BiLD Lab. Outside of the lab, I like to try new recipes, read lots of books, and go rock climbing!

title: Research
text:
I use a combination of behavioral and neurocognitive methods (mainly EEG)to study how we learn, understand, and communicate novel meanings using language. Specifically, I am interested in topics such as novel word learning, creative language production, and novel metaphor comprehension. I focus on monolingual as well bilingual populations, and have a particular interest for second language learning and comprehension.


My dissertation investigates how we learn new words. Specifically, I conducted a two-session study in which participants learned new words on Day 1 then returned to the lab on Day 2 to learn more words. They were then tested on both sets of words. I compared words learned today with words learned yesterday to investigate the effects of overnight consolidation on word learning. I compared two types of novel words—ones that could readily connect to existing concepts and ones that could not—to test the boundaries of the Complementary Learnings System theory of overnight consolidation. Additionally, I investigated word learning in two populations, English monolinguals learning novel words in their native language English, and Spanish-English bilinguals learning novel words in their L2, and investigated individual differences in both groups.



One of the unique aspects of human language is that we can communicate an infinite number of of ideas, including novel or creative ideas. I worked on an NSF-funded research project to investigate the neural processes underlying creative language production, specifically investigating the effect of supportive or unsupportive feedback on participants’ creative performance. We found that after receiving feedback (whether supportive or unsupportive), participants produced fewer but more highly original responses and showed increased alpha power (associated with increased internally directed attention, supporting creative thinking processes). In further analyses, we found decreased beta-band activity before feedback only in the unsupportive condition, possibly reflecting increased cognitive stress and internally directed attention required to adjust performance in the post-feedback phase. Our manuscript was published in 2026 in Neuropsychologia. (Download here)



My masters work, published in 2025 in Psychophysiology, (Download here) investigated how Chinese-English bilinguals comprehend novel metaphors in their second language, English. We compared processing of literal, novel metaphorical, and anomalous sentences. Compared to monolingual English speakers (who show a typical “graded” N400 effect to these three sentence types, with larger N400 responses to anomalous than to novel metaphorical than to literal sentences), bilinguals processing in their L2 show only an N400 effect for anomalous sentences, with no difference between literal and novel metaphorical sentences. These results demonstrate that creative language processing (specifically novel metaphorical processing) may be more effortful in an individual’s second language.

title: Research
text:
Other Research
In addition other my main research line focusing on novel aspects of language use, I have conducted research in adjacent research areas as well.

Vocal Emotion Recognition
I received an NSF-funded Partnerships for International Research and Education Fellowship in Summer 2023 to conduct research for two months at Radboud University in Nijmegen, the Netherlands. Here I investigated emotion recognition in Dutch-English bilinguals’ first and second language, and two languages they did not know. We found that emotion recognition was equally high for Dutch and English, indicating that (contrary to popular theories of emotion recognition) emotion recognition can be learned to a high level even of a non-native speaker and individual who is not a member of a particular cultural in-group. This work is currently under review.


Bi-Dialectal Language Processing
As a graduate research assistant on an NSF-funded collaboration between my PI Janet van Hell and collaborator Abby Walker (Virginia Tech) I gained experience with EEG fieldwork! Over three years, I collected EEG data at Penn State and additionally, traveled to Southwest Virginia four times with our Mobile EEG System to conduct EEG studies at multiple locations around the SWVA area including at Virginia Tech and in community colleges and community event centers in various towns. This project is interested in differences in mono-dialectal versus bi-dialectal processing of Mainstream and Southern American English. We have published the results from the pilot study for this project here.

title: Teaching
text:
In addition to my research, I also enjoy teaching and mentoring. I have I taught an upper-level psychology class in Fall 2025 (~30 students) about my favorite subject—Language and Thought. This course covered fun topics such as language development, linguistic relativity, language and technology, bilingualism, and more! I am currently preparing to teach PSYCH 100 this fall (~350 students).
One of the many privileges of teaching psychology is that course content inherently and directly relates to individuals’ own experiences! As psychologists, and in psychology classes, we discuss questions that are fundamental to the human experience. My primary goal as an instructor is to foster a love of learning and sense of curiosity in my students. I aim to create classroom environments where students can develop a positive relationship with the learning process and learn to ask reflective and critical questions driven by their own interests and curiosity.

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching Research
Recently, I have begun to combine my interests in teaching and research. In collaboration with Teaching Professor Dr. Alicia Drais-Parrillo at Penn State, I have helped analyze data from a large-scale project on content-retention in psychology classes. This work has resulted in a conference talk at the Scholarship of Teaching and Learning Summit on the role of grades, interest, and study time on undergraduate psychology content-retention.

title: Teaching
text:
Mentorship
I am lucky to get to work with dozens of amazing undergraduate research assistants in the BiLD Lab. I have enjoyed getting to work closely with students who are invested in our ongoing research projects, such as two students who are currently trained to independently run the behavioral session of my dissertation experiment. I have also enjoyed mentoring students through the completion of their own research projects, from idea development to data analysis. I have mentored four students who developed their own research studies and conducted the research abroad independently (three in the Netherlands and one in Spain). Upon their return to the States after data collection, I mentored students through data analysis, and presenting their work at the undergraduate research fair. I especially enjoyed working with one of these students, who was highly motivated to pursue research, and additionally mentored her as she completed an undergraduate honors thesis using this research data, and is a co-author on one of my submitted manuscripts.
